Generated: 2026-07-09
Working title
Cellular and immunometabolic architecture of urinary tract infection susceptibility, recurrence, and pregnancy-associated inflammation: an integrative host-omics and single-cell validation study
Authors
Reuben S. Maghembe
Affiliations and co-author list to be finalized before journal formatting.
Keywords

urinary tract infection; recurrent UTI; pregnancy-associated UTI; UPEC; host omics; single-cell transcriptomics; innate immunity; NLRP3; TLR4; TLR5; glucocorticoid signaling; oxytocin signaling; immunometabolism
Abstract

Background: Urinary tract infection (UTI), recurrent UTI, and pregnancy-associated UTI involve pathogen exposure in host compartments where epithelial, immune, hormonal, inflammatory, and metabolic-response programs may shape disease expression. However, public omics datasets are often analyzed within individual compartments, limiting cross-context synthesis.

Methods: We constructed a curated UTI host-response module library and reanalyzed public datasets representing a recurrence-trigger bladder model, pregnancy-associated UTI tissues, a repaired human urine inflammatory comparator, and UPEC-responsive single-cell data. Matrices were inspected, repaired where possible, harmonized to gene-level features, and scored for modules capturing innate sensing, NF-kB/MAPK and interferon-like signaling, inflammatory cell death, neutrophil/NETosis, myeloid activation, immune restraint, hormonal signaling, oxidative stress, barrier repair, and transcriptionally inferred metabolic activity. Cross-cohort module priorities were integrated with sample-level and cell-distribution validation from GSE252321 single-cell data.

Results: The single-cell validation layer included 28313 cells, comprising 14857 control and 13456 UPEC-exposed cells across four biological samples. Cross-cohort integration identified 17 modules supported by both sample-level and cell-distribution single-cell validation. The strongest validated architecture involved NEUTROPHIL_NETOSIS, TYPE_I_INTERFERON_IRF, TLR4_LPS_SIGNALING, NLRP3_PYROPTOSIS, TLR5_FLAGELLIN_SIGNALING, NECROPTOSIS_MODULE, COMPLEMENT_COAGULATION_ANTIMICROBIAL, NF_KB_MAPK_INFLAMMATION, MACROPHAGE_DC_ACTIVATION, GLUCOCORTICOID_SIGNALING. Three modules, TCA_OXPHOS_MITOCHONDRIAL, APOPTOSIS_MODULE, MAST_CELL_MRGPRX2_AXIS, showed single-cell-consistent directionality that differed from the bulk-dominant cross-context direction, suggesting compartment- or cell-state-specific behavior.

Conclusions: This integrative host-omics atlas identifies convergent UTI inflammatory, hormonal, cell-death-associated, and transcriptionally inferred metabolic-response modules, while distinguishing broadly validated modules from context-dependent programs. The framework prioritizes mechanistic axes for future prospective, spatial, proteomic, metabolomic, and perturbational validation.
Introduction

Urinary tract infection is a common bacterial disease syndrome with substantial recurrence burden, and pregnancy-associated UTI is clinically important because infection-related inflammation occurs in a maternal-fetal physiological context. Although UPEC and other uropathogens are central to disease initiation, clinical outcome and recurrence risk are also shaped by host epithelial, immune, inflammatory, hormonal, and metabolic-response programs [Worby_2022_rUTI_multiomics; Hannan_2023_immune_defenses_urinary_tract; GSE280297_maternofetal_UTI_preterm; Schwartz_2023_uropathogen_host_pyelonephritis].

Existing omics studies provide valuable views of specific UTI-related compartments, including bladder tissue, urine, pregnancy-associated tissues, recurrence-trigger models, and single-cell immune states. However, these data are usually analyzed within individual datasets, limiting the ability to identify host-response modules that recur across biological contexts or distinguish broad inflammatory convergence from compartment-specific behavior [Worby_2022_rUTI_multiomics; Bandyopadhyay_2020_urinary_molecular_signature_sepsis; Liu_2025_CD137L_macrophage_UPEC_scRNA; GSE280297_maternofetal_UTI_preterm].

A module-level reanalysis strategy is well suited to this problem because it can compare interpretable host-response programs across heterogeneous platforms, organisms, tissues, and sample types. Here, curated modules were used to represent microbial sensing, NF-kB/MAPK and interferon-like signaling, inflammatory cell death, neutrophil and myeloid effector biology, immune restraint, pregnancy/hormonal signaling, oxidative stress, barrier repair, and transcriptionally inferred metabolic activity [Andreatta_2021_UCell; Wang_2024_scPS_benchmark; Andreatta_2026_UCell_pyUCell; Worby_2022_rUTI_multiomics].

The objective of this study was to reconstruct a multi-context UTI host-response atlas by integrating recurrence-trigger, pregnancy-associated UTI, human urine inflammatory comparator, and UPEC single-cell validation layers. The analysis aimed to identify convergent host-response modules, evaluate whether these modules were directionally supported in UPEC-responsive single-cell data, and define modules that behaved differently across bulk and single-cell contexts. The overall workflow is summarized in Figure 1.

The resulting atlas prioritized NEUTROPHIL_NETOSIS, TYPE_I_INTERFERON_IRF, TLR4_LPS_SIGNALING, NLRP3_PYROPTOSIS, TLR5_FLAGELLIN_SIGNALING, NECROPTOSIS_MODULE, COMPLEMENT_COAGULATION_ANTIMICROBIAL, NF_KB_MAPK_INFLAMMATION, MACROPHAGE_DC_ACTIVATION, GLUCOCORTICOID_SIGNALING as dominant single-cell-validated host-response modules. In contrast, TCA_OXPHOS_MITOCHONDRIAL, APOPTOSIS_MODULE, MAST_CELL_MRGPRX2_AXIS showed single-cell-consistent directionality that differed from the bulk-dominant cross-context direction, highlighting compartment- and cell-state-specific behavior. This framework provides a mechanistic scaffold for interpreting UTI host responses across bladder, urine, pregnancy-associated tissue, and single-cell immune contexts.
Methods
Study design and public dataset framework

This study used an integrative public-data reanalysis framework to reconstruct host-response programs associated with UTI-related inflammation, recurrence-associated bladder perturbation, pregnancy-associated UTI, human urine inflammatory states, and UPEC-responsive single-cell profiles. The analysis was organized around a bladder-blood-gut-pregnancy conceptual axis, but formal module scoring was restricted to datasets with usable gene-level expression or count matrices after matrix inspection and repair. Dataset acquisition, file-structure inspection, matrix repair, module scoring, cross-cohort integration, and single-cell validation were implemented as sequential reproducible phases within the project directory `project:`.

The final included analysis layers were: GSE186800 as a Gardnerella-triggered bladder recurrence model; GSE280297 gene-count data as a pregnancy-associated UTI model with bladder, uterus, and placenta contexts; GSE112098 as a repaired human urine inflammatory comparator; and GSE252321 as a single-cell UPEC validation layer. Datasets or files that could not be resolved to reliable gene-level features were held from formal module scoring. Specifically, the alternative GSE280297 normalized matrix was held because module coverage remained unsuitable after repair, and GSE261018 was held as diagnostic-only because the acquired count matrix lacked usable gene identifiers in the downloaded file. Additional single-cell resources identified during acquisition were reserved for possible later annotation or validation extensions.
Public data acquisition and file-structure inspection

GEO series matrices, supplementary files, and GEO file lists were downloaded or inventoried during the acquisition phases. Each candidate matrix was inspected for delimiter structure, header composition, sample-column identity, feature-column identity, row count, and compatibility with gene-symbol-level module scoring. Processed matrices with clear gene identifiers and sample columns were advanced to matrix repair and module scoring. Files with ambiguous feature identities, non-gene first columns, or unresolved orientation were not used for formal contrasts until repair could establish a usable gene-by-sample or gene-by-cell structure.
Curated UTI host-response module library

A curated host-response module library was constructed in GMT format and used consistently across bulk, urine, pregnancy-associated tissue, recurrence-trigger, and single-cell analyses. The library covered innate microbial sensing modules including TLR4/LPS and TLR5/flagellin signaling; inflammatory signal-transduction modules including NF-kB/MAPK and type-I-interferon/IRF programs; inflammatory cell-death modules including NLRP3/pyroptosis, apoptosis, necroptosis, and ferroptosis/oxidative cell death; effector and regulatory immune modules including neutrophil/NETosis, macrophage/DC activation, Treg/IL10/CD137, mast-cell/MRGPRX2, complement/coagulation/antimicrobial defense; pregnancy and hormonal modules including glucocorticoid and oxytocin/pregnancy signaling; oxidative stress and transcriptionally inferred metabolic activity modules; and urothelial barrier/repair modules. Module names were retained as stable computational identifiers, while biological interpretation used conservative language such as transcriptionally inferred metabolic activity rather than direct metabolic flux.
Matrix repair and gene-symbol harmonization

Each dataset was repaired using matrix-specific logic based on the observed file structure. For GSE186800, the raw gene CPM matrix was parsed to retain the true biological sample columns and remove non-sample annotation columns. For GSE280297, the gene-count matrix was repaired by mapping mouse Ensembl-like identifiers to gene symbols using locally available symbol relationships from the project data layer. The repaired GSE280297 gene-count matrix was used for tissue- and outcome-specific pregnancy-associated UTI contrasts, whereas the alternative normalized matrix was held because it did not recover adequate module gene coverage. For GSE112098, the GEO series matrix expression table was extracted and repaired by mapping probe identifiers to gene symbols using the independent validation matrix that contained probe-to-symbol annotations. Sample labels were inferred from GEO sample titles and characteristics to define sepsis and vascular-surgery control groups.

For GSE252321, the first direct attempt to download individual supplementary matrix files failed because the matrices were packaged inside the GEO RAW tar archive rather than exposed as standalone files. The RAW tar archive was therefore downloaded, and four expected matrices were extracted: Control_1, Control_2, UPEC_1, and UPEC_2. Matrix inspection showed that each extracted file was a gene-by-cell count matrix with gene symbols in the first column and cell barcodes in the remaining columns. These matrices were then advanced to memory-safe single-cell scoring.
Module scoring in bulk, urine, and pregnancy-associated tissue matrices

Repaired gene-by-sample matrices were transformed as appropriate for the expression scale of each dataset and then scored against the curated module library. Module scores were calculated only when sufficient module genes were present in the repaired matrix. Dataset-specific group contrasts were generated as signed module-score differences between predefined biological groups. GSE186800 was analyzed as Gardnerella versus PBS exposure in a recurrence-trigger bladder model. GSE280297 gene-count data were analyzed across bladder, uterus, and placenta compartments using contrasts for UTI89 preterm versus mock, UTI89 term versus mock, UTI89 preterm versus UTI89 term, and nonpregnant UTI89 versus mock where applicable. GSE112098 was analyzed as a human urine sepsis versus vascular-surgery control comparator after probe-to-symbol repair.

For each contrast, the analysis recorded group means, signed deltas, effect-size summaries, nominal P values where applicable, and Benjamini-Hochberg false-discovery-rate estimates. Because the included datasets differed in organism, tissue source, sample type, platform, and study design, formal integration emphasized module-level directionality, relative effect size, and cross-context concordance rather than direct gene-level meta-analysis.
Cross-cohort module atlas and prioritization

The recurrence, pregnancy-associated UTI, and human urine comparator contrasts were merged into a context-by-module atlas. Each module was summarized across contexts by the number of contributing contrasts, number of model families represented, mean and median absolute module-score delta, maximum absolute delta, mean absolute effect size, minimum P value, minimum FDR, positive and negative directional context counts, concordance fraction, and dominant direction. A cross-cohort priority score was then calculated to rank modules by effect-size magnitude, model-family breadth, and directional concordance. This generated the Phase U7 integrated module atlas and candidate cross-cohort host-response hierarchy.
Single-cell UPEC module scoring

The GSE252321 single-cell validation layer was processed from four extracted gene-by-cell count matrices. Each matrix was streamed in a memory-safe manner. In the first pass, per-cell total counts, detected genes, matrix gene counts, and module-gene coverage were calculated. In the second pass, counts were normalized within each cell by library size, scaled to 10,000 counts per cell, log-transformed using log1p, and summarized into module scores by averaging the normalized expression of present module genes. Per-cell module scores were retained in both wide and long formats, and per-sample mean and median module scores were calculated for Control_1, Control_2, UPEC_1, and UPEC_2.

Single-cell validation was evaluated at two levels. First, sample-level Control-versus-UPEC contrasts were calculated from the four biological sample summaries and treated as the primary replicate-aware single-cell summary. Second, cell-level Control-versus-UPEC contrasts were calculated across all cells and interpreted as a module-score distribution comparison. The cell-level statistics were not interpreted as independent biological-replicate tests because cells are nested within two control and two UPEC biological samples.
Integration of single-cell validation with the cross-cohort atlas

The Phase U9 integration step merged the cross-cohort priority table with the GSE252321 sample-level and cell-level single-cell module contrasts. For each module, the bulk-dominant cross-cohort direction was compared with the single-cell sample-level and cell-level UPEC-versus-control directions. Modules were classified as bulk-consistent sample-and-cell validated when both single-cell summaries matched the cross-cohort dominant direction. Modules with internally consistent single-cell directionality that differed from the cross-cohort dominant direction were classified as single-cell-consistent but bulk-dominant-discordant. These discordant patterns were treated as potential compartment- or cell-state-specific behavior rather than contradictions.
Figure generation and manuscript traceability

Draft heatmaps and barplots were generated during the analysis phases and exported in PNG, SVG, EPS, and high-resolution TIFF formats where applicable. A manuscript-facing figure map, results subsection outline, table-to-claim traceability table, and results claim index were created to ensure that each major manuscript statement could be traced to a specific output table and candidate figure. The Methods draft was similarly linked to computational phases, input files, and generated output tables through the Phase U12 methods traceability table.
Statistical and interpretive boundaries

The study was designed as an integrative public-omics reanalysis rather than a new prospective cohort. Therefore, results were interpreted as module-level convergence and validation across public datasets, not as causal evidence. Cross-dataset integration was performed at the module level because platform, organism, tissue, sample type, and study-design differences made direct pooled gene-level inference inappropriate. Single-cell cell-level statistics were interpreted as distributional validation because cells are nested within biological samples. Metabolic modules were interpreted as transcriptionally inferred metabolic activity rather than direct biochemical flux. Datasets lacking recoverable gene identifiers or adequate module coverage were held from formal scoring to preserve feature-level interpretability.
Results
Dataset repair and construction of a multi-context UTI host-response atlas

We first constructed a harmonized host-response module framework to compare UTI-related signals across recurrence, pregnancy-associated infection, human urine inflammatory states, and single-cell UPEC exposure. After matrix repair and module-score harmonization, the usable analysis framework comprised the GSE186800 recurrence-trigger bladder model, the GSE280297 pregnancy-associated UTI model, the repaired GSE112098 human urine comparator, and the GSE252321 single-cell UPEC validation layer. These layers were integrated into a module-level atlas in which each biological context was represented by signed module-score contrasts and then evaluated against UPEC-responsive single-cell module shifts (Figure 1; Figure 2; Table S1).

The single-cell validation layer included 28313 cells across four biological samples, comprising 14857 control cells from 2 control samples and 13456 UPEC-exposed cells from 2 UPEC samples. Because the design contains two biological samples per group, sample-level single-cell contrasts were treated as biological-replicate summaries, whereas cell-level contrasts were interpreted as validation distributions rather than independent biological-replicate tests (Figure 6; Table S2).
Recurrence-trigger and pregnancy-associated UTI models reveal recurrent innate inflammatory modules

In the GSE186800 recurrence-trigger bladder model, Gardnerella exposure produced module-level shifts involving MACROPHAGE_DC_ACTIVATION (decreased, delta -0.609); COMPLEMENT_COAGULATION_ANTIMICROBIAL (decreased, delta -0.391); NF_KB_MAPK_INFLAMMATION (increased, delta 0.322); FERROPTOSIS_OXIDATIVE_CELL_DEATH (increased, delta 0.314); TLR5_FLAGELLIN_SIGNALING (increased, delta 0.294); NEUTROPHIL_NETOSIS (decreased, delta -0.271). These results supported the use of GSE186800 as a recurrence-trigger context rather than as a stand-alone acute infection model (Figure 2; Table S3).

The GSE280297 pregnancy-associated UTI model added tissue-specific resolution across bladder, uterus, and placenta. The largest module shifts included NEUTROPHIL_NETOSIS in Bladder (Bladder_UTI89_Nonpregnant_vs_Mock_PBS, delta 1.061); TYPE_I_INTERFERON_IRF in Bladder (Bladder_UTI89_Preterm_vs_Mock_PBS, delta 0.976); NEUTROPHIL_NETOSIS in Bladder (Bladder_UTI89_Preterm_vs_Mock_PBS, delta 0.943); TLR5_FLAGELLIN_SIGNALING in Bladder (Bladder_UTI89_Nonpregnant_vs_Mock_PBS, delta 0.919); TLR4_LPS_SIGNALING in Bladder (Bladder_UTI89_Nonpregnant_vs_Mock_PBS, delta 0.894); GLUCOCORTICOID_SIGNALING in Bladder (Bladder_UTI89_Nonpregnant_vs_Mock_PBS, delta -0.890); TCA_OXPHOS_MITOCHONDRIAL in Placenta (Placenta_UTI89_Term_vs_Mock_PBS, delta -0.703); NF_KB_MAPK_INFLAMMATION in Bladder (Bladder_UTI89_Nonpregnant_vs_Mock_PBS, delta 0.699). Together, these contrasts captured innate-sensing, inflammatory cell-death, hormonal-response, and transcriptionally inferred metabolic activity axes in pregnancy-associated UTI contexts (Figure 5; Table S3).
A repaired human urine comparator supports inflammatory and immunometabolic module activation

Repair of the GSE112098 series matrix recovered a human urine inflammatory comparator with sepsis and vascular-surgery control groups. In this urine comparator, the strongest module differences included GLUCOCORTICOID_SIGNALING (delta 0.427, FDR 0.001); OXYTOCIN_PREGNANCY_SIGNALING (delta 0.343, FDR 0.001); GLYCOLYSIS_CARBON_FLUX (delta 0.575, FDR 0.001); NLRP3_PYROPTOSIS (delta 0.507, FDR 0.001); MACROPHAGE_DC_ACTIVATION (delta 0.470, FDR 0.004); NEUTROPHIL_NETOSIS (delta 0.404, FDR 0.005); NF_KB_MAPK_INFLAMMATION (delta 0.403, FDR 0.011); NECROPTOSIS_MODULE (delta 0.306, FDR 0.011); TLR4_LPS_SIGNALING (delta 0.293, FDR 0.011); APOPTOSIS_MODULE (delta 0.180, FDR 0.026). These results supported the inclusion of GSE112098 as a human urine validation context for inflammatory, cell-death, innate-sensing, and transcriptionally inferred metabolic activity modules (Figure 2; Figure 3; Table S4).
Cross-cohort prioritization identifies convergent UTI host-response modules

Cross-cohort prioritization identified a recurrent host-response architecture dominated by NEUTROPHIL_NETOSIS, TYPE_I_INTERFERON_IRF, TLR4_LPS_SIGNALING, NLRP3_PYROPTOSIS, TLR5_FLAGELLIN_SIGNALING, NECROPTOSIS_MODULE, COMPLEMENT_COAGULATION_ANTIMICROBIAL, NF_KB_MAPK_INFLAMMATION, MACROPHAGE_DC_ACTIVATION, GLUCOCORTICOID_SIGNALING, OXYTOCIN_PREGNANCY_SIGNALING, UROTHELIAL_BARRIER_REPAIR, FERROPTOSIS_OXIDATIVE_CELL_DEATH, OXIDATIVE_STRESS_NRF2, PENTOSE_PHOSPHATE_NADPH. These modules were prioritized because they combined cross-context representation, large module-score shifts, broad model-family coverage, and directional support in the single-cell UPEC validation layer. The highest-ranked modules concentrated in neutrophil/NETosis, interferon-like inflammation, TLR4/TLR5 innate sensing, NLRP3/pyroptosis, necroptosis, complement/antimicrobial defense, NF-kB/MAPK inflammation, macrophage/DC activation, glucocorticoid signaling, and oxytocin/pregnancy-linked signaling (Figure 2; Figure 3; Figure 4; Table S5).
Single-cell UPEC validation confirms dominant innate-sensing, inflammatory, and cell-death modules

Single-cell validation supported 17 modules in both sample-level and cell-distribution comparisons. The strongest validated modules were NEUTROPHIL_NETOSIS, TYPE_I_INTERFERON_IRF, TLR4_LPS_SIGNALING, NLRP3_PYROPTOSIS, TLR5_FLAGELLIN_SIGNALING, NECROPTOSIS_MODULE, COMPLEMENT_COAGULATION_ANTIMICROBIAL, NF_KB_MAPK_INFLAMMATION, MACROPHAGE_DC_ACTIVATION, GLUCOCORTICOID_SIGNALING. At the single-cell level, UPEC exposure was associated with higher module scores for neutrophil/NETosis, interferon-like inflammation, TLR4 and TLR5 signaling, NLRP3/pyroptosis, necroptosis, complement/antimicrobial defense, NF-kB/MAPK inflammation, macrophage/DC activation, glucocorticoid signaling, and oxytocin/pregnancy-linked signaling. These findings provide a cell-distribution validation layer for the bulk and cross-cohort atlas, while preserving the interpretation boundary that biological replication remains limited to two control and two UPEC samples (Figure 3; Figure 4; Figure 6; Table S6).
Discordant single-cell modules highlight compartment- and cell-state-specific behavior

Three modules showed single-cell-consistent directionality that differed from the bulk-dominant cross-context direction: TCA_OXPHOS_MITOCHONDRIAL, APOPTOSIS_MODULE, MAST_CELL_MRGPRX2_AXIS. These modules should be interpreted as context-dependent rather than contradictory. The integrated atlas combines bulk bladder tissue, pregnancy-associated tissues, human urine expression, recurrence-trigger models, and UPEC-responsive single-cell distributions. Therefore, discordant directionality may reflect compartment composition, cell-state enrichment, or differences between whole-tissue and single-cell immune compartments rather than failed validation (Figure 3; Figure 6; Table S7).
Results-section traceability note

All claims in this draft are linked to Phase U10 and Phase U11 traceability outputs. The major claim-level index is provided in `phaseU11_results_claim_index_v1.tsv`, and the key numerical anchors are provided in `phaseU11_key_numeric_anchors_v1.tsv`.
Discussion
Principal findings

This integrative reanalysis identified a validated UTI host-response architecture in which 17 modules were directionally supported by both cross-cohort and single-cell evidence [Worby_2022_rUTI_multiomics; Liu_2025_CD137L_macrophage_UPEC_scRNA; Bandyopadhyay_2020_urinary_molecular_signature_sepsis]. The highest-priority modules were NEUTROPHIL_NETOSIS, TYPE_I_INTERFERON_IRF, TLR4_LPS_SIGNALING, NLRP3_PYROPTOSIS, TLR5_FLAGELLIN_SIGNALING, NECROPTOSIS_MODULE, COMPLEMENT_COAGULATION_ANTIMICROBIAL, NF_KB_MAPK_INFLAMMATION, MACROPHAGE_DC_ACTIVATION, GLUCOCORTICOID_SIGNALING. Together, these modules define an inflammatory architecture centered on innate sensing, neutrophil and myeloid effector activity, inflammatory cell-death-associated programs, hormonal-response modules, oxidative stress, and transcriptionally inferred metabolic activity.
Innate sensing and signal-transduction architecture

TLR4/LPS and TLR5/flagellin signaling were among the strongest validated modules, accompanied by NF-kB/MAPK inflammation and interferon-like/IRF programs. This pattern supports a model in which UTI-related host response is not captured by a single inflammatory pathway but instead involves coordinated microbial sensing and downstream signal-transduction programs. The single-cell validation layer strengthens this interpretation because these modules increased in UPEC-exposed cells at both sample-summary and cell-distribution levels [Hannan_2023_immune_defenses_urinary_tract; Schwartz_2023_uropathogen_host_pyelonephritis; Russell_2023_UPEC_epithelial_trained_immunity].
Neutrophil, myeloid, and antimicrobial effector biology

Neutrophil/NETosis was the top-ranked validated module, and complement/coagulation/antimicrobial defense and macrophage/DC activation also ranked highly. These findings suggest that inflammatory effector recruitment, antimicrobial defense, and myeloid activation are central features of the cross-context UTI host-response atlas [Hannan_2023_immune_defenses_urinary_tract; Schwartz_2023_uropathogen_host_pyelonephritis; Liu_2025_CD137L_macrophage_UPEC_scRNA; Masajtis_Zagajewska_2017_NETs_UTI; Demirel_2023_NETs_children_UTI]. However, because module scores can reflect both cell abundance and per-cell transcriptional state, these signals should be interpreted as inflammatory program enrichment rather than direct measurement of cell-type proportions unless supported by later deconvolution or annotation layers.
Inflammatory cell-death-associated programs

NLRP3/pyroptosis, necroptosis, and ferroptosis/oxidative cell-death modules were recurrently prioritized. These modules provide a cell-injury and inflammatory cell-death-associated layer linking tissue inflammation, urine inflammatory states, pregnancy-associated contexts, and UPEC-responsive single-cell data [Demirel_2018_NLRP3_UPEC; Russell_2023_UPEC_epithelial_trained_immunity]. The results should be interpreted as transcriptional evidence for cell-death-associated programs rather than direct evidence of executed pyroptosis, necroptosis, apoptosis, or ferroptosis.
Pregnancy-associated hormonal and inflammatory modules

Glucocorticoid signaling and oxytocin/pregnancy signaling connected pregnancy-associated UTI contrasts with the broader single-cell validation architecture. This supports the idea that pregnancy-associated UTI risk and inflammatory response may involve interactions between innate immune activation and hormonal-response transcriptional programs [GSE280297_maternofetal_UTI_preterm; Solano_Arck_2020_steroids_pregnancy_fetal_development; Cain_Cidlowski_2017_immune_regulation_glucocorticoids; UvnasMoberg_2023_oxytocin_labor_peripartum]. These modules should be framed as transcriptional signatures of hormonal-response biology rather than direct hormone concentration measurements.
Transcriptionally inferred metabolic and oxidative-stress activity

Oxidative stress, pentose-phosphate/NADPH, glycolysis-associated, and TCA/OXPHOS modules contributed an immunometabolic layer to the atlas. These results should be described as transcriptionally inferred metabolic activity, not direct metabolic flux [Wang_2024_scPS_benchmark; Andreatta_2026_UCell_pyUCell; Andreatta_2021_UCell]. The TCA/OXPHOS module was especially informative because it showed single-cell-consistent directionality that differed from the bulk-dominant direction, suggesting that mitochondrial/TCA/OXPHOS-associated expression may depend strongly on compartment composition or UPEC-responsive cell state.
Directionally discordant modules and compartment specificity

Three modules were classified as single-cell-consistent but bulk-dominant-discordant: TCA_OXPHOS_MITOCHONDRIAL, APOPTOSIS_MODULE, MAST_CELL_MRGPRX2_AXIS. These patterns should not be presented as failed validation. Instead, they likely reflect the fact that the integrated atlas combines bulk bladder tissue, pregnancy-associated tissues, urine expression, recurrence-trigger models, and single-cell immune distributions. Directional discordance therefore becomes a useful signal for identifying modules whose behavior may be compartment-, cell-type-, or cell-state-dependent.
Limitations

This study is limited by its public-data reanalysis design, heterogeneity across species and platforms, differences in tissue and sample source, small biological replicate number in the GSE252321 single-cell validation layer, and the absence of direct metabolomics, proteomics for all contexts, or perturbational validation. The single-cell cell-level tests were interpreted as distributional validation because cells are nested within two control and two UPEC biological samples. Datasets without recoverable gene identifiers or adequate module coverage were held from formal scoring, which preserves interpretability but limits coverage of potentially relevant compartments.
Future directions

Future work should test the prioritized modules in prospective cohorts with matched urine, blood, bladder or exfoliated urothelial compartments, pregnancy-specific sampling, pathogen metadata, recurrence follow-up, and orthogonal protein or metabolite measurements. Single-cell or spatial validation with explicit cell-type annotation would help distinguish cell-abundance effects from per-cell transcriptional activation. Perturbational models targeting TLR4/TLR5, NF-kB/MAPK, NLRP3, glucocorticoid signaling, oxytocin-associated signaling, and oxidative-stress/metabolic-response modules would be particularly valuable.
Concluding model

Overall, the atlas supports a model in which UTI-related host response is organized around microbial sensing, inflammatory signal transduction, neutrophil and myeloid effector activity, inflammatory cell-death-associated programs, hormonal-response modules, and transcriptionally inferred metabolic and oxidative-stress activity. Single-cell validation strengthens the dominant inflammatory architecture while also identifying modules whose directionality is likely compartment- or cell-state-dependent.
Data availability

All analyses were performed using publicly available datasets. Included datasets were GSE186800, GSE280297 gene-count data, GSE112098, and GSE252321. Dataset-specific inclusion and holding decisions are summarized in Table S1. Processed output tables, module-score summaries, figure-generation reports, and manuscript traceability files were generated within the project directory.
Code availability

The analysis was implemented as sequential project phases using reproducible scripts stored under `04_scripts/`. Key phases covered dataset acquisition, matrix repair, module scoring, cross-cohort integration, single-cell validation, manuscript traceability, and manuscript drafting.
Ethics statement

This study reanalyzed publicly available de-identified datasets and did not generate new human participant data. Final journal-specific ethics language should be aligned with source dataset access terms and target-journal policy.
Author contributions

Reuben S. Maghembe conceived the study, designed the computational workflow, performed the integrative analysis, interpreted the results, and drafted the manuscript. Final author-contribution statements should be updated after co-author review and contribution confirmation.

Figure 1. Multi-context host-omics framework for reconstructing urinary tract infection host-response architecture.

Figure 1. Multi-context host-omics framework for reconstructing urinary tract infection host-response architecture. Public datasets representing a recurrence-trigger bladder model, pregnancy-associated UTI tissues, a repaired human urine inflammatory comparator, and UPEC-responsive single-cell immune data were organized into a harmonized module-scoring workflow. Matrices were inspected and repaired where possible, harmonized to gene-level features, and scored using a curated UTI host-response module library. Cross-cohort module priorities were integrated with sample-level and cell-distribution single-cell UPEC validation to identify broadly supported and context-dependent host-response modules.
Figure 2. Integrated cross-cohort module atlas.

Figure 2. Integrated cross-cohort module atlas. Context-by-module heatmap showing signed module-score deltas across recurrence-trigger, pregnancy-associated UTI, and repaired human urine comparator contexts. The figure highlights recurrent but context-dependent directionality across innate sensing, inflammatory cell-death, myeloid, neutrophil, hormonal, oxidative-stress, and transcriptionally inferred metabolic activity modules.
Figure 3. Cross-cohort atlas with single-cell UPEC validation.

Figure 3. Cross-cohort atlas with single-cell UPEC validation. Integrated heatmap combining cross-cohort module deltas with GSE252321 single-cell UPEC-versus-control validation deltas. Sample-level single-cell contrasts summarize two control and two UPEC biological samples, whereas cell-level contrasts are interpreted as validation distributions because cells are nested within biological samples.
Figure 4. Prioritized single-cell-validated UTI host-response modules.

Figure 4. Prioritized single-cell-validated UTI host-response modules. Ranked barplot of modules prioritized by integrated cross-cohort and single-cell validation support. Top modules include neutrophil/NETosis, type-I-interferon/IRF, TLR4/LPS, NLRP3/pyroptosis, TLR5/flagellin, necroptosis, complement/coagulation/antimicrobial defense, NF-kB/MAPK, macrophage/DC activation, glucocorticoid signaling, and oxytocin/pregnancy signaling.

Figure 6. Single-cell UPEC validation layer.

Figure 6. Single-cell UPEC validation layer. GSE252321 single-cell module-score validation across Control_1, Control_2, UPEC_1, and UPEC_2 matrices. UPEC-exposed cells showed higher module scores for innate sensing, neutrophil/NETosis, NLRP3/pyroptosis, necroptosis, interferon-like, NF-kB/MAPK, glucocorticoid, and oxytocin/pregnancy-linked modules, while TCA/OXPHOS showed lower single-cell directionality relative to controls.

Figures
Figure 1

Figure 1. Multi-context host-omics framework for reconstructing urinary tract infection host-response architecture. Public datasets representing a recurrence-trigger bladder model, pregnancy-associated UTI tissues, a repaired human urine inflammatory comparator, and UPEC-responsive single-cell immune data were organized into a harmonized module-scoring workflow. Matrices were inspected and repaired where possible, harmonized to gene-level features, and scored using a curated UTI host-response module library. Cross-cohort module priorities were integrated with sample-level and cell-distribution single-cell UPEC validation to identify broadly supported and context-dependent host-response modules.
Figure 2

Figure 2. Integrated cross-cohort module atlas. Context-by-module heatmap showing signed module-score deltas across recurrence-trigger, pregnancy-associated UTI, and repaired human urine comparator contexts. The figure highlights recurrent but context-dependent directionality across innate sensing, inflammatory cell-death, myeloid, neutrophil, hormonal, oxidative-stress, and transcriptionally inferred metabolic activity modules.
Figure 3

Figure 3. Cross-cohort atlas with single-cell UPEC validation. Integrated heatmap combining cross-cohort module deltas with GSE252321 single-cell UPEC-versus-control validation deltas. Sample-level single-cell contrasts summarize two control and two UPEC biological samples, whereas cell-level contrasts are interpreted as validation distributions because cells are nested within biological samples.
